The Africa News Edition
Complete Regional Briefing
News That Matters to Africa • "Overlooked and Misunderstood"
Thursday, 09 July 2026
From The Desk Of The Editor — Quote of the Day
"Our world is starving for the genuine after feasting too long on deception."
Eastern Africa
DRC: Ebola Health Crisis
DRC • HEALTH
Ebola Health-Workers Go On Strike Over Pay

The healthcare workers at the epicenter of Congo's Ebola outbreak are walking off their jobs to protest delays in their payments, threatening efforts to slow the outbreak that officials said continues to spread faster than the response. The latest government data shows 1,708 recorded cases, including 580 deaths, and that the first month of this Ebola outbreak was already the worst on record, health authorities said. The strike comes at the start of enrollment for clinical trials for the treatment of the Bundibugyo virus that is responsible for this outbreak.

Source: AP
DRC • HEALTH
Ebola Outbreak Continues To Expand As Infections Rise

Health authorities in the DRC say the country's Ebola outbreak remains in an expansion phase, with confirmed infections and deaths continuing to increase despite an extensive public health response. The WHO said population movements, insecurity in affected areas and delayed reporting are complicating efforts to break transmission chains. Vaccination campaigns, surveillance and contact tracing remain under way, but officials caution that the outbreak has yet to stabilize.

Source: Reuters
DRC • HEALTH
Residents in Eastern Congo Cling to Hope as a New Ebola Treatment Trial Begins

Residents at the epicenter of Congo's Ebola outbreak are pinning their hopes on experimental treatments after researchers began a highly anticipated study in early July of two possible Ebola treatments in hopes of fighting the still-growing outbreak. At the Ebola treatment center inside Bunia's Evangelical Medical Center, in eastern Congo's Ituri province, the launch of the research was marked by urgency rather than ceremony on Thursday...the research effort unfolded quietly alongside the daily struggle to keep patients alive. The virus causing this outbreak, called Bundibugyo, is less common than others that cause Ebola disease and there are no specific treatments or vaccines for it.

Source: AP
Health workers at Bunia's Evangelical Medical Center work to contain the Bundibugyo outbreak—the first month of which has already become the worst on record for Ebola in DRC.
DRC: Constitutional Tensions
DRC • POLITICS
Opposition Grows to Tshisekedi's 3rd Term Bid

The opposition in the DRC has called demonstrations in the capital and other cities to demand the resignation of President Tshisekedi, who it says is trying to cling on to power by changing the constitution. Political tensions have grown recently, more than two years before the vast central African country, beset by decades-long conflict and an Ebola outbreak in its east, goes to the polls. The dispute now focuses on a bill setting out the framework for a referendum on constitutional change, which was adopted last month in parliament, where the presidential bloc holds an overwhelming majority.

Source: AFP
ETHIOPIA: Child Soldiers Crisis
ETHIOPIA • CONFLICT
Ethiopia Faces Renewed Child Soldier Crisis in Tigray

Ethiopia is facing renewed concerns over the recruitment of child soldiers in Tigray, a development that could undermine fragile peace efforts in the northern region. The concerns were raised in a Human Rights Watch report, following earlier accusations by Addis Ababa itself that the Tigray Defence Force had been forcing civilians to join its ranks. Human Rights Watch said children as young as 15 had been forced to fight since April, when the armed wing of the Tigray People's Liberation Front (TPLF) resumed clashes with government forces. Those being forced into the fight include former combatants who laid down their arms after a peace deal in 2022. The campaign has also targeted men and boys on the street, in offices and during night-time house-to-house searches, as well as at gold-mining sites, the rights watchdog said.

Source: The East African
KENYA: Governance & Rights
KENYA • GOVERNANCE
Heavy Police Deployment Prevents Saba Saba Protests From Gaining Momentum

Kenya marked this year's Saba Saba anniversary under an extraordinary security operation as thousands of police officers sealed off key roads leading to Nairobi's central business district and government buildings. The annual commemoration, which honours the 1990 pro-democracy movement, had been expected to draw demonstrations over governance, corruption and the rising cost of living. Instead, the overwhelming police presence discouraged large gatherings before they could take shape. Opposition figures accused the government of using intimidation to suppress legitimate dissent, while authorities insisted the measures were necessary to protect lives and property. The tense day underscored the continuing political friction between President William Ruto's administration and critics demanding greater political accountability.

Source: AP
KENYA • RELIGIOUS FREEDOM
Kenyan Rastafarians hope court will rule they can smoke cannabis legally

Rastafarians in Kenya are waiting with baited breath for a high court ruling later this month which will determine whether they can smoke cannabis legally. Its use is central to their religion, and is often smoked in communal rituals to enhance meditation, promote inner peace, and bring them closer to "Jah" or God. The landmark ruling on the lawsuit filed by the Rastafari Society of Kenya in 2021 to decriminalize the use of cannabis for "spiritual" purposes is due to be pronounced on 15 July. While the movement is recognised in Kenya, a 1994 law makes possession of weed punishable by 10 years' imprisonment and a substantial fine.

Source: AfricaNews
RWANDA • INTERNATIONAL RELATIONS
Kigali questions UN inquiry methods on eastern DR Congo

Rwanda has challenged the methodology of the UN commission investigating human rights abuses in eastern DR Congo, arguing that its findings rely on unverified allegations rather than independently corroborated evidence. Addressing the 62nd session of the UN Human Rights Council in Geneva, Rwanda's Permanent Representative, Ambassador Urujeni Bakuramutsa, said serious accusations should be supported by transparent sourcing, objective verification, and credible investigations. She criticized the commission's reliance on anonymous testimonies, selectively cited open-source material, and accounts that had not been independently verified. Bakuramutsa also questioned the commission's ability to fulfil its mandate after it acknowledged difficulties accessing North and South Kivu—the conflict's epicentre—and instead conducted much of its work from Kinshasa. Without field visits, direct engagement with affected communities, and on-the-ground verification, she argued, the inquiry cannot produce definitive or impartial conclusions, urging the Human Rights Council to ensure its decisions rest on credible and balanced evidence.

Source: New Times
SOMALIA • POLITICS
Puntland and Jubaland Reject Turkey-led Mogadishu Talks

Puntland and Jubaland have declined to take part in meetings hosted by Turkish intelligence officials in Mogadishu, questioning Ankara's neutrality in the country's ongoing political disputes. The two federal member states argued that Turkey could not be considered an impartial mediator in discussions over Somalia's key political issues, including the electoral process, accusing Ankara of providing direct support to the federal government in Mogadishu. The decision comes amid heightened tensions between the Federal Government of Somalia and opposition groups over the country's political future and election arrangements.

Source: Garowe Online
SOUTH SUDAN • CONFLICT
Govt Official Assassinated

A government-appointed county commissioner in an opposition stronghold in South Sudan's oil-rich Jonglei State was "assassinated" by opposition forces in renewed violence, the government confirmed late Monday. Fighting in Jonglei has intensified in recent days, with an unknown number of casualties. The strategic county of Akobo has seen the appointment of both government-backed and opposition-backed county commissioners.

Source: AP
SUDAN: Escalating Humanitarian Crisis
SUDAN • MILITARY
RSF mobilizes to besiege city

The Rapid Support Forces (RSF) have mobilized more of their troops to attack El Obeid, the capital of North Kordofan state in south-central Sudan, despite international warnings against targeting the city, which is home to about half a million people, according to the United Nations, including more than 80,000 displaced people who fled violence in other parts of Sudan. The RSF said a column from its Kordofan sectors was advancing toward El Obeid as part of what it described as hundreds of moving units heading toward the city.

Source: Sudan Times
SUDAN • HUMAN RIGHTS
UN Orders Urgent Inquiry Into Killings Around Sudan's El-Obeid

The UN Human Rights Council has voted to establish an urgent investigation into alleged atrocities committed around El-Obeid, where fighting between the Sudanese Armed Forces and the Rapid Support Forces has intensified. The resolution condemns reports of indiscriminate attacks on civilians, destruction of infrastructure and widespread abuses as the conflict spreads deeper into Kordofan. Khartoum rejected parts of the resolution, arguing that it infringes on Sudan's sovereignty, while rights groups welcomed the move as a step toward accountability.

Source: Reuters
SUDAN • HUMANITARIAN CRISIS
Fighting Intensifies Around El-Obeid As Humanitarian Crisis Deepens

Sudan's conflict has entered another dangerous phase as fierce fighting continues around El-Obeid, the capital of North Kordofan State. The Sudanese Armed Forces and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces have exchanged heavy artillery and ground assaults in and around the city, forcing more civilians to flee. Aid organisations warn that access to food, medical care and humanitarian assistance is becoming increasingly difficult as insecurity spreads across the region. International concern has grown over reports of civilian casualties and attacks on essential infrastructure. Diplomatic efforts to revive peace negotiations have made little progress, leaving millions caught in one of the world's largest displacement crises.

Source: Reuters
"El-Obeid lies at the intersection of roads linking central Sudan with Darfur and southern states, making it one of Sudan's most important commercial centers and a key logistics hub for military operations and humanitarian aid."
SUDAN • STRATEGIC ANALYSIS
Why El-Obeid Matters as Sudan's War Enters a New Phase

For much of Sudan's three-year civil war between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF), international attention has centred on Khartoum and the Darfur region. In recent weeks, however, attention has increasingly shifted to El-Obeid as fighting has intensified across Kordofan, prompting warnings from UN officials and humanitarian organisations that another acute humanitarian emergency could be unfolding.

El-Obeid, the capital of North Kordofan state, lies about 360 km southwest of Khartoum at the intersection of roads linking central Sudan with Darfur and the country's southern states. That location has made it one of Sudan's most important commercial centres and a key logistical hub for both military operations and humanitarian aid. The city has remained under the control of the SAF, making it one of the army's most important positions in western Sudan.

Military analysts say control of el-Obeid helps shape movement along key supply routes connecting central Sudan with Kordofan and Darfur, helping explain why both the SAF and the RSF consider it strategically important. The battle for el-Obeid reflects a broader shift in Sudan's war. After the SAF regained territory in and around Khartoum earlier this year, fighting increasingly concentrated in western Sudan, particularly across the Kordofan and Darfur regions.

Source: Al Jazeera
El-Obeid has emerged as the epicenter of Sudan's western campaign, with over 500,000 residents now facing intensified fighting and humanitarian collapse.
KENYA & TANZANIA • GOVERNANCE
Kenya, Tanzania Suppress Protests with Heavy Police Deployments

Heavy police deployments in the capitals of Kenya and Tanzania suppressed planned protests on Tuesday, with July 7 a significant date for the East African neighbors. In Kenya, the day commemorates the 1990s struggle for multiparty democracy. In Tanzania, it marks the founding of what would become the ruling party 72 years ago. Tanzania's protests were meant to call for democratic reforms following the disputed October election and to demand the release of opposition leader Tundu Lissu, who is jailed on treason charges. A few people were arrested as police in Kenya's capital, Nairobi, cordoned off parliament buildings with barbed wire and blocked major roads. Businesses were closed. Kenyan opposition politician James Orengo described the police presence in Nairobi as an effort to scare citizens.

Source: AP
North Africa
LIBYA • DIPLOMACY
Pakistan Quietly Mediates Between Libya's Rival Administrations

Pakistan has begun discreet diplomatic efforts to bridge the divide between Libya's rival eastern and western authorities, according to officials familiar with the initiative. The mediation seeks to encourage dialogue between competing political and military leaders as Libya continues to struggle with institutional division and periodic violence more than a decade after the fall of Muammar Gaddafi. While neither Libyan administration has publicly commented on the talks, the initiative reflects growing interest from non-traditional diplomatic actors in supporting efforts to stabilize the country. Success remains uncertain, but observers say any sustained dialogue could help reduce tensions and revive momentum towards a broader political settlement.

Source: Reuters
MOROCCO • INSTITUTIONAL
Cardinal Resigns Over Abuse Allegations

Spanish Cardinal Cristobal Lopez Romero, who is also the Archbishop of Rabat, Morocco, has suspended himself after being accused of molesting women, the Vatican said on its own news site. "I am accused of inappropriate behaviour toward adult women," Lopez Romero said in a statement published by Vatican News, adding that the situation had prompted the Catholic Church to open a preliminary investigation. Romero closed by stating: "I have committed neither assault nor violence nor sexual harassment." The cardinal pledged to fully cooperate with the Vatican investigation, and said that to avoid interfering with it, he would refrain from presiding over public celebrations and participating in pastoral activities.

Source: Reuters
Southern Africa
ESWATINI • MIGRATION
Fourth group of deportees from US arrive in Eswatini

Eleven more migrants deported from the United States have arrived in Eswatini as part of President Donald Trump's wide-ranging immigration crackdown. A US immigration attorney familiar with some of the cases says the group includes at least two who have legal protection that advocates say should shield them from deportation. It is the latest expulsion in line with the Trump administration's unprecedented "third-country" deal that has already seen 19 people sent to Eswatini since July 2025. Two of them - a Jamaican and Cambodian - have been repatriated, but 17 remain in a high-security prison, held without charge. It is not yet clear what are the nationalities of the 11 who arrived in Eswatini on Wednesday.

Source: AfricaNews
SOUTH AFRICA: Anti-Migrant Violence Crisis
SOUTH AFRICA • REGIONAL RELATIONS
Ghana tells Ramaphosa 'Don't Come Visit – Yet' over anti-migrant violence

Ghana has deferred next month's bilateral commission meeting with South Africa, saying the recent wave of anti-migrant violence would overshadow discussions intended to strengthen relations between the two countries. Government spokesperson Felix Kwakye Ofosu said Accra believed it was more appropriate to meet once tensions had eased. The commission was due to be co-chaired by President John Dramani Mahama and South African President Cyril Ramaphosa. Ghana recently repatriated hundreds of its citizens following threats against foreign nationals, while reports that a Ghanaian citizen died during the unrest have heightened public concern. Both governments say they remain committed to maintaining strong diplomatic relations despite the postponement.

Source: Reuters
NIGERIA & SOUTH AFRICA • DIPLOMATIC CRISIS
Nigeria Protests Deaths Of Its Citizens During Anti-Migrant Violence

Nigeria says two of its citizens were killed during the recent wave of anti-migrant violence in South Africa, including one who allegedly died following police action. Abuja has demanded a full investigation and warned that the attacks risk damaging relations between the two countries if those responsible are not held accountable. South African authorities say they are investigating the incidents while continuing security operations in areas affected by unrest. The violence, fueled by anti-immigrant sentiment, has drawn widespread criticism across Africa and renewed concerns about the safety of foreign nationals living and working in South Africa.

Source: Reuters
SOUTH AFRICA • SECURITY
Government Maintains Security Operation As Anti-Migrant Tensions Persist

South African authorities continue to maintain heightened security across several provinces following weeks of anti-migrant protests that have targeted foreign-owned businesses and communities. Police and security personnel remain deployed in identified hotspots to prevent further violence while investigations continue into deaths and attacks reported during the unrest. The protests have attracted criticism from governments across Africa and raised concerns about South Africa's regional standing.

Source: Reuters
Security deployments continue across South African hotspots as regional governments demand accountability for deaths and attacks on foreign nationals.
KENYA & SOUTH AFRICA • EVACUATION
151 Kenyans evacuated from South Africa amid xenophobic violence, intimidation

At least 151 Kenyans have been evacuated from South Africa as the government continues efforts to repatriate nationals from the country amid xenophobic violence and acts of intimidation. In a statement, Prime Cabinet Secretary and Cabinet Secretary for Foreign and Diaspora Affairs, Musalia Mudavadi, said the evacuation is being carried out in a safe, orderly and timely manner in collaboration with the South African government. He added that the Kenyan High Commission in Pretoria is working closely with South African authorities to facilitate the return of Kenyans wishing to come home while ensuring the welfare and security of those remaining in the country. As of July 2, a total of 240 Kenyans had registered with the High Commission for assistance.

Source: Eastleigh Voice
SOUTH AFRICA • MUNICIPAL FINANCE
Treasury Holds Back Funds For Irregularities

South Africa's National Treasury will withhold funding from Johannesburg and dozens 69 municipalities for at least a month over persistent and serious non-compliance with financial management regulations, officials said on Wednesday. Treasury's deputy director general of intergovernmental relations explained that R3.6 billion rand in funding would be withheld from Johannesburg in July, but stressed the move did not amount to placing the city under administration, and that the same action applied to many other local governments. Finance Minister Enoch Godongwana has repeatedly raised concerns about Johannesburg's financial management.

Source: NewsDay – SA
ZIMBABWE • CONSTITUTIONAL POLITICS
President Signs Extension To His Term

President Emmerson Mnangagwa has signed into law constitutional changes extending his time in office to 2030. The legislation, approved by Parliament last month, also introduces a provision allowing future presidents to be elected by lawmakers instead of a direct public vote. President Emmerson Mnangagwa has signed legislation extending his tenure by two years, allowing him to remain in office until 2030. Government spokesperson Nick Mangwana confirmed the move on Tuesday, announcing on social media that the bill had been "signed, sealed and delivered" after receiving presidential assent.

Source: Africa News
ZIMBABWE • INFRASTRUCTURE
Power Restored After Nationwide Blackout Disrupts Country

Zimbabwe's state electricity utility has restored power to most parts of the country after a technical fault on a key transmission line triggered a nationwide blackout. The outage disrupted businesses, transport services, telecommunications and government operations, exposing the fragility of the country's ageing electricity infrastructure. Engineers worked through the night to reconnect the national grid while authorities investigated the cause of the failure. Although electricity has largely returned, officials warned that further maintenance will be required to improve network reliability. Zimbabwe has struggled for years with chronic power shortages, ageing infrastructure and limited generating capacity, forcing many businesses to rely on private generators.

Source: Reuters
West Africa
MALI: Security Crisis & Infrastructure
MALI • CONFLICT
Coordinated Insurgent Attacks Expose Widening Security Crisis

Armed groups launched coordinated attacks on military positions across several parts of Mali, including targets in the north, centre and south of the country, in one of the most extensive offensives seen in recent months. The assaults, claimed by insurgent groups operating in the Sahel, struck army bases and other security installations, underscoring the persistent threat facing the ruling military government despite intensified counter-insurgency operations. The Malian army said it had repelled several attacks and inflicted heavy losses on the assailants, while security operations continued in affected areas. The renewed violence highlights the deteriorating security environment as militant groups continue to expand their reach despite years of military campaigns and changing international security partnerships.

Source: Reuters
MALI • INFRASTRUCTURE
Sabotage Plunges Bamako into Water and Power Crisis

Large parts of Mali's capital have been left without electricity and running water after suspected sabotage damaged a key power transmission line. The disruption has deepened hardship for residents as insecurity and jihadist attacks continue to strain the country's fragile infrastructure. Bamako has endured widespread electricity and water outages for two days after suspected sabotage disrupted a major power transmission line supplying the city, according to utility officials. The infrastructure disruption comes as the Group for the Support of Islam and Muslims (JNIM) has intensified pressure on routes leading into Bamako. For months, the jihadist group has imposed blockades on major roads and set fire to buses, cargo trucks and fuel tankers in an apparent effort to weaken the country's economy and disrupt supply chains.

Source: AFP
NIGERIA: Governance & Regulation
NIGERIA • ANTI-CORRUPTION
President orders probe into fake government agency

President Bola Tinubu on Tuesday ordered the country's anti-corruption agency to launch a comprehensive investigation into the activities of the "Presidential Foreign Intervention Promotion Council" (PFIPC), a fictitious body that was recently discovered to have been operating as a federal government agency. The agency doesn't exist under Nigerian law. Yet it was set to receive about 1.3 billion naira ($944,300) in this year's national budget. Its offices were reportedly located inside the federal secretariat in Abuja, the same complex that houses genuine government ministries. According to the statement, a Nigerian citizen identified as Adeniyi Adeyemi Matthew falsely presented himself as the director-general of the purported agency and claimed to be a presidential appointee.

Source: Xinhua
NIGERIA • TECH REGULATION
Tinubu Orders Investigation Into Tech Giants Over News Content

President Bola Tinubu has instructed Nigeria's competition regulator to investigate major international technology companies over the alleged unauthorized use of Nigerian news content and possible anti-competitive practices. The Federal Competition and Consumer Protection Commission will examine whether digital platforms have benefited commercially from news produced by Nigerian media organisations without fair compensation. The move reflects growing pressure from governments worldwide seeking a more balanced relationship between technology companies and news publishers. Authorities say the investigation will determine whether existing competition and consumer protection laws have been breached before deciding on any regulatory or legal action.

Source: Reuters
SENEGAL • POLITICS
President Faye Moves To Launch His Own Political Party

President Bassirou Diomaye Faye is preparing to establish a new political party, marking the clearest sign yet of his political break with former Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko. A coalition supporting the president said the new movement is intended to give Faye an independent political base after months of tensions within the ruling alliance. The split has fueled speculation over the future of one of Africa's most closely watched reform governments, elected on promises to tackle corruption, create jobs and strengthen state institutions. While no timetable has been announced for the party's formal launch, the move is expected to reshape Senegal's political landscape ahead of future elections.

Source: Reuters
Continental News
PAN-AFRICAN • GEOPOLITICS
Why some African nations are turning down Trump aid money

The Trump administration is offering hundreds of millions of dollars in new health assistance to African countries after dismantling much of the previous U.S. foreign aid architecture. Unlike earlier aid programmes, however, the new agreements are explicitly tied to American strategic and commercial interests, including requests for access to health data, disease samples, and, in some cases, preferential treatment for U.S. interests in critical minerals. Kenya became the first country to sign a $2.5 billion package last year, although its implementation has been delayed by legal challenges over privacy concerns. Several governments—including Ghana, Zimbabwe and Zambia—have resisted or rejected the proposed deals. Ghana and Zimbabwe cited concerns over data sovereignty and the lack of guarantees that medical discoveries derived from African biological samples would benefit their own populations, while Zambia objected to linking health funding with mineral access negotiations.

The Trump administration insists that U.S. assistance is "strategic capital" rather than charity, reflecting its broader "America First" approach, but critics argue the conditions risk undermining trust and treating vital public health cooperation as a geopolitical bargaining tool.

Source: BBC
2026 World Cup Special
World Cup Controversy
2026 WORLD CUP • CONTROVERSY
Egypt's Bitter Exit Sparks Fury Over VAR and FIFA

Egypt's dramatic 3–2 defeat to Argentina in the Round of 16 has ignited a storm of controversy, with players, coaches, pundits, and sections of the international media questioning both key refereeing decisions and the expanding influence of VAR. Egypt's exit was one that left the Pharaohs "with a bitter taste despite breaking new ground." Leading 2–0 with less than half an hour remaining, Egypt looked poised to eliminate the reigning world champions before the match turned on a highly contentious VAR intervention. Mostafa Zico appeared to have extended Egypt's lead, only for his goal to be ruled out after a lengthy review over what officials deemed a foul by Marwan Attia on Lisandro Martínez during the build-up. Zico would score minutes later, but by then Argentina had mounted a remarkable comeback to claim victory. Egypt also protested what they believed was an unpunished foul on Mohamed Salah in the closing moments before Argentina scored the decisive goal. While many acknowledged that awarding a penalty would have been a soft decision, the sequence of events only deepened Egyptian frustration.

The controversy quickly overshadowed the football itself. Egypt manager Hossam Hassan openly questioned whether external pressure had influenced the officials. "In football, there are sometimes external factors that go beyond the technical aspects. The world champions received support at every level." Hassan suggested that FIFA wanted Lionel Messi and the defending champions to remain in the tournament, while reports claimed José Mourinho privately described the match as "daylight robbery."

The New York Times' refereeing analysis was equally damning. It argued that Marwan Attia's challenge on Lisandro Martínez amounted to routine contact, occurring nearly 100 yards from goal, and should never have warranted overturning the goal. The paper described the VAR intervention as "an astonishing overreach" that contradicted the principle that only "clear and obvious errors" should be corrected.

As Argentina completed their comeback, emotions boiled over. The referee issued a string of yellow cards to Egypt, including bookings for goalkeeper Mostafa Shobeir, Hamdi Fathy, Marwan Attia, and even head coach Hossam Hassan. Adding to the ill feeling, an Argentina supporter positioned above the players' tunnel reportedly waved an Israeli flag toward Hassan as he left the field—an apparent attempt to provoke the Egyptian manager.

The Guardian argued that the incident reflected broader concerns surrounding FIFA under Gianni Infantino, warning that increasingly interventionist officiating risks undermining confidence in the sport itself. The newspaper suggested that when controversial decisions appear to shape outcomes rather than simply enforce the Laws of the Game, football begins to resemble "scripted entertainment" rather than genuine competition.

For Egypt, the disappointment runs deeper than elimination. Having stood within touching distance of one of the greatest World Cup upsets in history, the Pharaohs left Atlanta believing they had been denied not only a place in the quarter-finals, but also a fair chance to finish the job.

Sources: Reuters, The New York Times, The Guardian, AP
Egypt's controversial exit against Argentina sparked international debate over VAR integrity and FIFA's role in protecting the reigning champions.
